coredata.dc:title: Machine learning assisted dual-emission fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array detection of multiple antibiotics under stepwise prediction strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Zijun
Wang, Kejia
Zhang, Mengqian
Wang, Tianhao
Du, Xuejun
Gao, Zideng
Hu, Shuwen
Ren, Xueqin
Feng, Haojie
coredata.dc:description: Effective sensors to detect antibiotics as environmental and health hazards are urgently needed. Herein, we constructed a dual-emission fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array based on novel high fluorescence quantum yield carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots. Multi-dimensional data (fluorescence intensities and maximum emission wavelengths) was used to establish a sensor array platform with improved specificity. To meet the challenges of establishing a unified model and detecting outside datasets samples, we innovatively built a unified SX-model using a “stepwise prediction” strategy combined with machine learning to screen optimal methods. By integrating classification and concentration models under a tree-based pipeline optimization technique framework, the extreme random forest was selected as the most accurate classification model. The sensor array detected nine antibiotics at 0.5–50 μM with 95% accuracy and 4.93% average concentration error for unknown samples outside the datasets. Simultaneous identification of binary and ternary mixed samples was also enhanced. Furthermore, antibiotics in 216 river water and milk samples were discriminated with 100% accuracy and 3.25% and 4.43% average concentration errors of unknown samples outside the datasets, respectively. Finally, antibiotics were completed visually identified. The proposed original SX-model assisted dual-emission sensor not only overcomes low specificity and immobility, but breaks the bottleneck of existing analysis methods showing great application potential in the array detection field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual emission sensor array
Antibiotics
Stepwise prediction
Machine learning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of P-CDs and CdTe QDs
Detection of antibiotics with the P-CDs/CdTe QDs sensor array
Preparation and characterization of P-CDs and CdTe QDs
Differentiation of binary and ternary mixtures with SX-model processing
Visual assay of antibiotics by experiment and image digitization using a color-scanning application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (Inset: size distribution of the P-CDs); (b) and HRTEM images; (c) FT-IR spectrum; (d) XPS spectrum of the P-CDs.
(a) Accuracy of two constructed models from different data sets (I, I+λ); (b) the eigenvalue importance.

Identification of nine antibiotics using the SX-model in deionized water (a) 3-dimensional diagram of prediction results; (b) classified prediction confusion matrix heat map; (c) the linear fitting curve relationship between the true category and predicted category of unknown samples; (d) the linear fitting curve relationship between the true concentration and predicted concentration of unknown samples.

Identification of binary and ternary mixtures using the SX-model in deionized water (a) 3-dimensional diagram of prediction results; (b) the classified prediction confusion matrix heat map; (c) the linear fitting curve relationship between the true category and predicted category of unknown samples; (d) the linear fitting curve relationship between the true concentration and predicted concentration of unknown samples.

Identification of the nine antibiotics using the SX-model in river water (a) 3-dimensional diagram of prediction results; (b) the classified prediction confusion matrix heat map (c) the linear fitting curve relationship between the true category and predicted category of unknown samples; (d) the linear fitting curve relationship between the true concentration and predicted concentration of unknown samples.

Visualization results the nine antibiotics using the SX-model in river water (a) 3-dimensional diagram of prediction results (15 μM); (b) the classified prediction confusion matrix heat map (15 μM); (c) 3-dimensional diagram of prediction results (30 μM); (d) the classified prediction confusion matrix (30 μM).
Schematic representation of dual emission sensor array for the establishment and detection of antibiotics.

head.ce:title: Machine learning assisted dual-emission fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array detection of multiple antibiotics under stepwise prediction strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment Sciences, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Farmland Soil Pollution Prevention and Remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Farmland Soil Pollution Prevention and Remediation, Beijing 100193, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Efficient Utilization of Soil and Fertilizer Resources, College of Resources and Environment Sciences, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Efficient Utilization of Soil and Fertilizer Resources, College of Resources and Environment Sciences, Shandong Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Laboratory for Efficient Utilization of Soil and Fertilizer Resources, College of Resources and Environment Sciences, Shandong Agricultural University, Tai’an 271018, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Resources and Environment Sciences, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources and Environment Sciences, China Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective sensors to detect antibiotics as environmental and health hazards are urgently needed. Herein, we constructed a dual-emission fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array based on novel high fluorescence quantum yield carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots. Multi-dimensional data (fluorescence intensities and maximum emission wavelengths) was used to establish a sensor array platform with improved specificity. To meet the challenges of establishing a unified model and detecting outside datasets samples, we innovatively built a unified SX-model using a “stepwise prediction” strategy combined with machine learning to screen optimal methods. By integrating classification and concentration models under a tree-based pipeline optimization technique framework, the extreme random forest was selected as the most accurate classification model. The sensor array detected nine antibiotics at 0.5–50 μM with 95% accuracy and 4.93% average concentration error for unknown samples outside the datasets. Simultaneous identification of binary and ternary mixed samples was also enhanced. Furthermore, antibiotics in 216 river water and milk samples were discriminated with 100% accuracy and 3.25% and 4.43% average concentration errors of unknown samples outside the datasets, respectively. Finally, antibiotics were completed visually identified. The proposed original SX-model assisted dual-emission sensor not only overcomes low specificity and immobility, but breaks the bottleneck of existing analysis methods showing great application potential in the array detection field.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics, which are mainly secondary metabolites produced by bacteria and other microorganisms or their artificial analogs, are used to inhibit the outbreak of animal diseases and bacterial infections [1]. Due to their low cost, high antimicrobial activity, and hypotoxicity, antibiotics are widely used in biological research, agriculture, animal husbandry, and the food industry [2]. However, the disproportionate global use of antibiotics in recent years has resulted in many side effects that cannot be ignored. Antibiotic residues will eventually enter the human body through water and the food chain, resulting in adverse physiological effects such as hearing loss, renal toxicity, and allergic reactions. Furthermore, the excessive accumulation of antibiotics and their residues in the environment and animals promotes bacterial resistance [3,4].

Many techniques for the determination of antibiotics have been developed based on approaches such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [5], high-performance liquid chromatography [6], capillary electrophoresis [7], chemiluminescence [8], molecularly imprinted polymers [9], and fluorescent chemosensors [10]. The fluorescent sensing has become widespread because it is inexpensive, simple, fast, sensitive, and capable of real-time online detection [11]. However, owing to the complexity of substances found in the environment, traditional fluorescence sensors may face challenges in detecting a variety of antibiotics. Therefore, array detection as an alternative method has attracted attention owing to its high accuracy, variability, simplicity, rapidity, and high selectivity.

A sensor array simulates the mammalian olfactory system to simultaneously detect and distinguish a series of analytes with similar structures and properties [12,13]. However, most array detection methods have adopted the enhanced or weakened fluorescence intensity of a single fluorophore as the response signal, which can easily be affected by many factors and cause measurement error [14]. Moreover, some inherent issues with sensor selectivity, visualization, and mobile analysis must be solved. Compared with single-emission sensors, dual-emission sensors with two different fluorescent units are superior in that they provide multiple emission bands [15]. Furthermore, dual-emission sensors can eliminate fluorescence intensity fluctuations through self-calibration to provide more accurate results in practical applications [1,16,17].

Choosing proper fluorescent materials for sensor elements is key to building sensors for pollutant detection. To date, various types of materials (e.g., quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), metallic nanoclusters (NCs) have been used to create dual-emission sensors [18–20]. CDs are an attractive fluorescent indicator because of their excellent qualities, including their strong fluorescence, variable raw materials, stable fluorescence, water solubility, and biocompatibility [21,22]. Semiconductor QDs have the unique properties of tunable emission wavelengths, water solubility, and long fluorescence lifetimes [23–25]. Equipped with their fascinating chemical and optical capabilities, CDs and semiconductor QDs have been used as sensor elements to design dual-emission sensors [26].

Methods for data analysis in array detection have thus far included principal component analysis (PCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), support vector machine (SVM), and artificial neural networks (ANNs) [27–30]. PCA and LDA are statistical techniques that are used to reduce the dimensionality of datasets. However, they have limitations in solving linear inseparable and nonlinear classification problems [31,32]. SVM is a supervised pattern classification algorithm that has been applied to linear inseparable and nonlinear classification problems, but it is suitable for processing small sample datasets [33]. ANNs are a powerful computing model; however, their complex nature limits their interpretability [34]. Long et al. established a sensor array with two kinds of CDs and PCA to establish individual models for different concentration gradients (1, 35, 45, 100, and 300 μM) to identify four antibiotics [35].

The various but discrepant analytical methods for sensor array detection have given rise to an urgent demand for the rapid selection of appropriate approaches and optimal models. Furthermore, current reported models have been built separately for different concentrations, and thus there is a lack of unified models. Therefore, array detection cannot be applied in real environments because there is no appropriate model for processing and analyzing the responses of unknown samples. The lack of a description of the logical relationships between samples in current models is another challenge that can result in failure to identify samples outside the dataset. Moreover, because uncertain factors and the samples outside the dataset remain unrecognized, these models lack generality and are challenging to apply to broader research. To extend the applications and prospects, it is necessary to build a unified model that focuses on logical relationships between samples to quickly screen for optimal analysis and data processing methods and correctly identify unknown samples outside the dataset.

In this work, we selected novel P-CDs and CdTe QDs to design a dual-emission fluorescence/ colorimetric sensor array based on the inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching, and electrostatic interactions to distinguish between nine antibiotics: ofloxacin (OFLX), amikacin sulfate (AMK), pefloxacin mesylate (PF), norfloxacin (NFC), kanamycin sulfate (KNM), doxycycline (DOX), metacycline (MTC), tetracycline (TC), and streptomycin (SM) ( Scheme 1). The P-CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield were synthesized using peanut shells as the raw material. The favorable response between the antibiotics and dual-emission sensor caused changes in fluorescence intensity (FI) and maximum emission wavelengths (MEW) as the antibiotics were added to the system. According to the FI in the presence and absence of antibiotics (I and I 0, respectively) and the MEW of the front and rear peaks (λ
01
− λ
1 and λ
02
– λ
2 , respectively), a fluorescence analysis pattern was created for each antibiotic. Then, the stepwise prediction strategy combined with machine learning was introduced for the first time, and a unified SX-model was established by integrating classification and concentration models under the framework of the tree-based pipeline optimization technique (TPOT). Additionally, the optimal classification model was selected through TPOT, and the multidimensional data obtained by the array were further analyzed and processed by the SX-model. The results showed that the nine antibiotics at different concentrations could be separated and differentiated into binary and ternary mixtures. In addition, the sensor array also identified a total of 112 unknown samples with a 98.21% classification accuracy and an average concentration error of only 4.93% for samples outside the datasets in deionized water. Together, these results demonstrate the excellent stability and feasibility of the P-CDs/CdTe QDs sensor array for the simple, rapid, and real-time visual discrimination and accurate quantification of multiple antibiotics. The SX-model in this study is the first of its kind built to analyze multidimensional data from dual-emission sensor arrays. Overall, compared with previous array analysis methods, the SX-model offers a powerful solution that surmounts the bottleneck of screening optimal methods by establishing a unified model and detecting samples outside the dataset.

OFLX, AMK, PF, NFC, KNM, sulfadimethoxine (SMM), sulfisoxazole (SS), ampicillin (AMP), and sulfapyridine (SP) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. DOX, trichloroacetic acid, and MTC were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). TC, SM, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl, KCl, CaSO4.2 H2O, AlCl3.6 H2O, and CoCl2.6 H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Peanut shells were purchased from Qingdao Lianfeng Agricultural Products Co., Ltd., and 96-well microplates were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents purchased from commercial suppliers were analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. Milk was purchased from a local supermarket, and deionized water was supplied by China Agricultural University (Beijing, China). A dialysis bag (MWCO, 2000 Da) was purchased from Biotopped Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) was used to acquire all fluorescence spectra, and a Tu-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer (General Analysis, China) was used to record UV spectra. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired with an iS10 Thermo Nicolet spectrometer (USA) using the KBr pellet sample preparation method. An advanced data acquisition and processing system was used to analyze the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS; Thermo Escalab 250Xi, USA) to maximize the amount of extracted information. The morphology and microstructure of the P-CDs were measured by high-resolution scanning/transmission electron microscopy (FEI Talos F200 series, 200 kV), and the quantitative determination of antibiotics was performed by triple quadrupole liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS, Agilent 1260/6465, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of P-CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using peanut shells as the carbon source, P-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. The peanut shells were first pre-oxidized for 400 ℃ at a rate of 10 ℃/min for 4 h in a muffle furnace under an oxygen-limited atmosphere. The pretreated peanut shell powder (0.3 g), citric acid (0.15 g, 0.71 mmol), and an ethylenediamine solution (150 μL, 2.2 mmol) were immersed in 15 mL of deionized water. The solution was sealed in a furnace and heated to initiate the hydrothermal reaction for 8 h at 180 ℃, then allowed to cool to room temperature. After filtering the reaction solution to remove the suspended solids, the P-CDs solution was dialyzed against deionized water using a dialysis bag (2000 Da) for 10 h to remove interfering substances. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of P-CDs was measured as described in Supporting information 1.1.

CdTe QDs were obtained following previously reported procedures with slight modifications [15,24]. In brief, 57.10 mg (0.5 mM) of CdC12.5 H2O and 120 μL (3.50 mM) of thioglycolic acid were mixed in 45 mL of deionized water, causing a white precipitate to immediately appear. Under magnetic stirring, 0.1 mol⋅L−1 NaOH was used to adjust the pH of the solution to above 10. As the pH increased, the solution became transparent. The solution was deoxygenated with N2 for 30 min at 80 ℃, and then 5 mL of NaHTe was rapidly injected under vigorous stirring. A solution of water-soluble CdTe QDs was subsequently obtained by refluxing with N2 at 80 ℃ for 40 min. The solution was purified with a dialysis membrane (2000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antibiotics with the P-CDs/CdTe QDs sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: P-CDs and CdTe QDs were prepared and utilized as sensing elements to fabricate a sensor array. The differentiation of nine antibiotics (OFLX, AMK, PF, NFC, KNM, DOX, MTC, TC, and SM) at concentrations of 500 nM and 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, and 50 μM were added to a P-CDs (15 μL, 50 μg mL−1)/CdTe QDs (60 μL, 5 μM) sensor solution. Distilled water was then added to maintain a total sample volume of 1.0 mL. Antibiotics in binary and ternary mixtures were also analyzed. TC and OFLX were selected to prepare binary mixtures with molar ratios of 0/100, 20/80, 30/70, 40/60, 60/40, 70/30, 80/20, and 100/0, with the total concentration of the two antibiotics, maintained at 25 μM. For the ternary mixtures, MTC, OFLX, and AMK were mixed according to molar ratios of 40/40/20, 30/30/40, 20/20/60, 10/10/80, and 0/0/100 with the total concentration of the three antibiotics maintained at 25 μM. To further investigate the selectivity of the sensor array, the fluorescence response toward several interfering substances that are common in nature were measured, including the common inorganic ions Ca2+, Al3+, Ba2+, CO2+, K+, and Hg2+. The antibiotics SP, SS, AMP, and SMM were also selected to determine the specificity of the sensor array, and interference tests for the mixtures of antibiotics and coexisting substances were performed under the same conditions as for the initial antibiotic experiments.

After incubation, the emission spectra of the mixtures were recorded by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence intensities at 437 and 534 nm were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, and the difference in FI between the signals measured in the presence and absence of antibiotics was used as the fluorescence response. The fluorescence spectra were bimodal, with a front and rear peak. The FIs of the front and rear peaks are denoted as I 1 and I 2, respectively. I and I 0 represent the FI in the presence and absence of antibiotics, respectively, and the equation (I 0 − I)/I 0 was used to calculate the fluorescence response. The I 0 was measured as a blank measurement in each part of the experiment. In addition to the different FIs for the various antibiotics, the MEW also exhibited diverse changes. The sum of λ
01
− λ
1 and λ
02
− λ
2 was used to represent the MEW response, where λ
01 and λ
1 represented the MEW before and after the reaction for the front peak, respectively, and λ
02 and λ
2 represented the MEW before and after the reaction for the rear peak, respectively. Theλ
01 and λ
02 were measured as blank in all experiments. To verify the predictive ability and reliability of the sensor array, seven replicates were measured for each experimental condition. Hence, a training data matrix (3 dimensions × 9 antibiotics × 7 replicates) was developed, which was further analyzed in Python version 3.6.

River water and milk were selected as representative environmental samples. The river water was acquired from the Jing-Mi diversion canal (Beijing, China), and milk was purchased from a local supermarket (Beijing, China). The water sample was centrifuged at 12 000 rpm for 5 min and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove insoluble impurities and obtain the supernatant. The milk sample was pretreated according to reported methods. Briefly, trichloroacetic acid was added to the milk to remove biomacromolecules. After extraction by sonication for 20 min, the precipitated proteins were separated and removed by centrifugation (12 000 rpm, 10 min). Subsequently, the supernatant was obtained by filtering with a 0.22 µm membrane to remove impurities. The treated water and milk samples were diluted 10 times using deionized water. Then, the nine antibiotics were added to the real samples at concentrations of 5, 10, 20, and 30 μM, and distilled water was added to maintain a total sample volume of 1.0 mL. Using an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, the fluorescence spectra produced by the reaction of the well-mixed samples were recorded. Each concentration of the nine antibiotics was measured six times in parallel [35,36].

First, the CdTe QDs (12 μL, 5 μM) and P-CDs (3 μL, 50 μg mL−1) were added to 96-microwell plates. The nine antibiotics at concentrations of 15 and 30 μM were added to the plates. The river water was then added to maintain a total sample volume of 200 μL. The naked-eye detection of the fluorescence color change was carried out under excitation with a 312 nm UV lamp. Moreover, the fluorescence color can be digitized and output by using a developed color-scanning application (APP: World of Color, Maarten Zonneveld), which can be readily downloaded online by smartphones. Finally, the red (R), green (G), and blue (B) values were used to quantitatively analyze the antibiotics. Each concentration was measured eight times in parallel [37].

The array sensor detection data were processed and analyzed by a machine learning method with Python 3.6. The extreme random forest (ERF) algorithm is an ensemble of unpruned classification or regression trees induced from the bootstrap samples of training data using random feature selection in the tree induction process. The ensemble prediction is made by aggregating for improved accuracy and efficiency and exhibits a favorable performance compared with other existing algorithms. By building a random tree to separate attributes and generate random threshold values for every attribute, ERF combines the down-sampling majority class technique and ensemble learning idea, and classes are represented in each tree by changing the class distribution. The training set was randomly selected from the original sample set T, and the allowance function was obtained as follows [38]:
mg (X, Y) = avk I(h
k (X) = Y) －max j‡k av k
I(h
k (X) = j),
where mg (X, Y) represents the difference between the average number of correct and false classifications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of P-CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, P-CDs and CdTe QDs were used to construct a dual-emission fluorescence sensor array to detect nine antibiotics. The morphological, optical, and structural properties were characterized by TEM, UV absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, XPS, and FT-IR. The P-CDs exhibited a spherical structure, high mono-dispersity, and relatively uniform size (in the range of 1.0–4.5 nm), with a mean diameter of 2.25 nm ( Fig. 1a). The mean diameter of CdTe QDs is 3.29 nm (Fig. S1). In addition, clear crystal lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.2 nm were observed in the HRTEM image of the P-CDs (Fig. 1b), which is consistent with the literature. As depicted in Fig. 1c, the FT-IR spectrum of the P-CDs revealed absorption peaks corresponding to O–H/N–H stretching vibrations at 3411 cm−1, suggesting an adequate water solubility. In addition, the peak at approximately 1656 cm− 1 and 1389 cm− 1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO and N-CO bands. The peak located at 1555 cm− 1 is assigned to vibrational absorption of CC band [39–41], the FT-IR spectrum of the CdTe QDs revealed an absorption peak corresponding to O–H stretching vibrations at 3428 cm−1, and the peaks at approximately 1573 cm− 1 and 1383 cm− 1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO bands (Fig. S2) [42]. Full-scan XPS spectra were employed to explore the chemical composition and bonding states of the P-CDs. Three peaks were identified at approximately 284.8, 399.9, 531.3 eV related to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, which further confirmed the presence of C, N, and O in the P-CDs nanostructure (Fig. 1d) [43,44], Fig. S3 shows the XPS survey spectra of C1s, O1s, S 2p, S 2 s, Cd 3d, and Te 3d of CdTe QDs [42].

To be suitable for practical sensing applications, P-CDs and CdTe QDs should exhibit good fluorescence stability. Therefore, we investigated the influence of different parameters on their fluorescence stability. The following experiments were performed at pH 7.0 as that is the maximal fluorescence intensity of P-CDs and CdTe QDs (Fig. S4 and S5). The fluorescence intensity of P-CDs and CdTe QDs remained high and stable under excitation at 320 nm, scanned every 20 min for 2 h (Fig. S6 and S7). These results suggest that P-CDs and CdTe QDs can be used as reliable sensors.

Moreover, the optical properties of the P-CDs, including the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, were investigated. Under ambient light, the P-CDs solutions were colorless. As shown in Fig. S8, the P-CDs solutions emitted strong blue fluorescence under irradiation with 312 nm UV light. The maximum excitation and emission peaks were measured at 350 and 437 nm, respectively. The QY of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was 55.7%, and the QY of the P-CDs measured in an aqueous solution was 23%, as shown in Fig. S9, indicating a high photoluminescence QY and excellent optical properties.

To construct the dual-channel fluorescent sensor array, P-CDs and CdTe QDs were used as the two independent sensor elements. The FI and MEW response of the P-CDs/CdTe QDs complex system containing nine antibiotics (OFLX, AMK, PF, NFC, KNM, DOX, MTC, TC, and SM) at different concentrations were measured. Our studies investigated the variation in the FI ((I 0 − I)/I 0) and MEW (λ
0
– λ) responses of the P-CDs and CdTe QDs to establish an array detection platform. As shown in Fig. S10, the maximum excitation wavelength for the quinolones (Qs) containing OFLX, NFC, and PF was approximately 320 nm. In addition, the excitation spectra of the P-CDs and CdTe QDs exhibited favorable overlap with the tetracyclines (TCs) containing TC, MTC, DOX absorption spectra at 320 nm (Fig. S11 and S12). Thus, 320 nm was selected as the optimal maximum excitation wavelength for a sensitive response.

To further verify the detection mechanism, the excitation and absorption spectra of the nine antibiotics, P-CDs, CdTe QDs, and their mixtures were measured. As shown in Fig. S11, the excitation spectrum of the P-CDs overlapped significantly with the absorption spectrum of TCs (TC, MTC, DOX) at 320 nm, indicating the possibility of IFE. Furthermore, based on Fig. S13, it was further confirmed that the absorption spectrum of the P-CDs did not change with the addition of TCs, indicating that no complex formed between the two species. Therefore, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching after TCs addition is IFE [45,46]. As illustrated in Fig. S13 d), e), and f), the absorption spectrum of the P-CDs changed significantly with the addition of the aminoglycosides (AGs) containing AMK, SM, and KNM, indicating the formation of a stable complex to cause static quenching [47,48]. Because the Qs (OFLX, NFC, PF) themselves exhibit fluorescence and different Qs have different FIs and MEWs (Fig. S14), the front peaks exhibited both red and blue shifts.

Similarly, there was a good overlap between the absorption spectra of TCs and Qs and the excitation spectrum of the CdTe QDs, as shown in Fig. S6, and the absorption spectrum of the QDs did not change after TCs or Qs addition, indicating that no complexes formed (Fig. S15). Therefore, the CdTe QDs were quenched by IFE with the addition of TCs and Qs [49]. A previous study proved that AGs have positive surface charges in aqueous solutions [50]. After adding the AMK, SM, and KNM at 20 μM, the zeta potential of the aqueous dispersions increased from − 18.4 to − 3.78, − 8.19, and − 10.77 mV, respectively, as shown in Fig. S16, indicating that the negatively charged CdTe QDs interacted with the AGs via electrostatic interactions. As a result, the degree of fluorescence quenching differed for different analytes and concentrations. Based on these different response patterns, a dual-channel sensor array can be constructed to simultaneously identify the nine selected antibiotics.

Selectivity is a crucial index for estimating the detection capability of sensor arrays. To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the dual-emission sensor, experiments were conducted to replicate the complexity of real environments. Common metal ions (Ca2+, Al3+, Ba2+, CO2+, K+, and Hg2+) and various antibiotics (SP, SS, AMP, and SMM) as interfering substances were added to the detection system. As portrayed in Fig. S17, no significant signal changes were observed upon the addition of these interfering substances, indicating that they had little to no influence on the detection function of the sensor array. The satisfactory analytical performance of the dual-emission sensor array for antibiotic detection confirmed its feasibility, high sensitivity, and selectivity for application in complex environments.

The current data analysis methods for array detection are confronted with three challenges: 1) selecting the optimal model from the various machine learning methods; 2) the lack of unified models for practical application; and 3) identifying unknown samples with concentrations outside the datasets. These challenges lead to sensor array detection that is severely limited or not even applicable. Firstly, we proposed a stepwise prediction strategy combined with machine learning to initially establish the SX-model, which corrects defects of the traditional method. The SX-model is composed of two parts, a classification model and nine concentration models, as shown in Fig. 2. After the unknown samples were input into the SX-model, they were labeled by the classification model. Unknown samples were then categorized into their corresponding concentration model according to their label to obtain the concentration information. In previous array detection studies, models were established separately at each concentration for the analyte, causing unknown samples to be passed to the inappropriate model and remain unidentified. In this study, the classification model is uniquely established by removing the concentration labels of FI and MEW data. Therefore, unknown samples can be directly brought into the classification model. Thus, the array detection can be applied in practice. Secondly, the lack of description of logical relationships between samples in traditional machine learning results in failure to identify samples outside the dataset. The concentration model was established using FI data as a module to depict the logical relationship between samples in this study. A regression model between the concentration of each antibiotic and the measured FI data was established. Therefore, the challenge of identifying unknown samples with concentrations outside of the datasets was effectively solved. Finally, the classification and concentration models used TPOT to select the optimal machine learning method from various available ones (SVM, Random Feature Attention (RFA), ERF, gradient boosted decision tree, etc.). The problem of selecting the optimal model from the various machine learning methods was well tackled. Based on the TPOT pipeline, the SX-model was no longer a fixed model and could be automatically optimized according to the input data, having the potential to be extended for increased array detection.

When the test data were input into the SX-model, they first passed the classification model to obtain the appropriate labels, then entered the corresponding concentration model. Thus, the classification and concentration information of the test samples were exported simultaneously to realize sample identification. Through TPOT screening, ERF was adopted as the data processing method in the classification model and Ada boost regressor and K-neighbors regressor were selected as the data processing methods in the concentration model. Model parameters are listed in Table S1.

In past studies, only the FI data were used to build the detection models. To enhance our sensor array response signal for discriminating antibiotics, we added the MEW data for model construction because both the emission intensity and emission wavelength showed distinct variations for different antibiotics. The FI model and FI + MEW model results were compared to select the more accurate option for discriminating and quantitatively analyzing multiple antibiotics in different environments. As shown in Fig. 3a, the average accuracy of the model using FI data was 91.547%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.495%. In contrast, the accuracy of the model using both FI and MEW data was improved to 94.353%, with an RSD of 0.613%. It is obvious from the order of eigenvalue importance that the MEW data are the most important factor for enhancing the detection accuracy (Fig. 3b). In this work, the sensor array can generate a multidimensional response signal including both FI data ((I 0 − I)/I 0) and MEW data (λ
01
− λ
1 and λ
02
− λ
2 ) outputs (Fig. S18 to S20). Therefore, employing the SX-model with multidimensional datasets is feasible to quantify various antibiotics.

The analytical performance of the model for the measurement of nine single antibiotics in deionized water was first investigated by the qualitative analysis of eight concentrations (500 nM, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, and 50 μM). The measurements were repeated seven times for each analyte to enhance the detection accuracy. A total of 504 multidimensional data were used to build the SX-model, the accuracy of which was 95%, as shown in Figs. 4a and 4b. To test the ability of our sample to distinguish unknown samples, 112 unknown samples were also analyzed; the classification accuracy was 98.21% (Table S2), and a linear relationship was observed between the true category and predicted category with R 2 of 0.9888 (Fig. 4c). Next, the concentration accuracies for samples inside and outside the dataset were 100% and 83.33%− 111.11%, respectively (Tables S3). The R 2 value of the linear fitting curve representing the relationship between the true and predicted concentrations from 0 to 50 μM was 0.9988 (Fig. 4d). The aforementioned results show that this model can be used to quantify single antibiotics in deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of binary and ternary mixtures with SX-model processing
ce:section.ce:para: After successfully using our sensor array to detect nine single antibiotics, we further applied it to the simultaneous detection of binary and ternary mixtures samples to simulate the complexity of real environmental samples. As depicted in Fig. S21, the MEW was the most important eigenvalue, even more so for the complex samples than the single-species samples, proving that the addition of MEW data effectively improved the array detection accuracy. Popularizing the detection mode of fluorescence and wavelength double response is helpful to improve the array detection accuracy.

In the following work, binary mixtures of TC/OFLX at molar ratios of 0/100, 20/80, 30/70, 40/60, 60/40, 70/30, 80/20, and 100/0 and ternary mixtures of MTC/AMK/OFLX at molar ratios of 40/40/20, 30/30/40, 20/20/60, 10/10/80, and 0/0/100 were prepared in deionized water. The total concentration of each sample was maintained at 25 μM. For both the binary and ternary mixtures, all 78 samples with different proportions could be completely separated, resulting in 100% accuracy ( Figs. 5a and 5b). 20 unknown binary and ternary samples were also analyzed with a classification accuracy of 100%, and the corresponding relationship between the true and predicted categories was linear with an R 2 of 1 (Fig. 5c). The concentration accuracy for samples in the dataset was 100%, and the concentration accuracies for binary and ternary mixtures outside the dataset were 93.30–106.70% and 80.00–106.67%, respectively (Table S4 and S5). As shown in Fig. 5d, the 20 unknown samples were all distributed on the linear fitting curve between the true and predicted concentrations, with R 2 of 0.9981 and 0.9954 for binary and ternary samples, respectively. These high prediction accuracies further demonstrated that our sensor array could simultaneously detect binary and ternary mixture samples and thus manage complex situations.

We further validated the discriminative ability of the sensor array by testing real samples. A total of 216 real samples were prepared by spiking different concentrations (5, 10, 20, and 30 μM) of the nine single antibiotics into collected river water and milk. As shown in Figs. 6a, 6b, and S22, all the samples of river water and milk were correctly identified using the SX-model with 100% accuracy. Next, 52 unknown samples were selected to test the classification and concentration accuracy of the model, in which 18 samples were outside the dataset. The corresponding curves of the true vs. predicted categories displayed a linear relationship with an R 2 of 1, illustrating a 100% classification accuracy (Fig. 6c and S22c). In addition, the concentration accuracy of samples in the dataset was 100%, and the concentration accuracies of river water and milk samples outside the dataset were 92.95%− 104.17% and 97.59%− 109.38%, respectively (Tables S6 to S9). The R 2 values of the linear fitting curves between the true and predicted concentrations were 0.9952 and 0.9944 for river water and milk, respectively (Fig. 6d and S22 d). The aforementioned results demonstrate the excellent feasibility of our sensor array for discriminating unknown samples.

Similarly, 48 multidimensional data were selected from binary mixtures of TC and OFLX in river water and milk, and a 100% classification accuracy was obtained, as shown in Fig. S23. Furthermore, all unknown samples were correctly identified with a 100% classification accuracy and the R 2 of the linear fitting curve between the true and predicted concentrations was 0.9957 and 0.9994 for river water and milk, respectively (Fig. S24). The concentration accuracies for real river water and milk samples outside the dataset were 92.97%− 110.55% and 97.37%− 103.94%, respectively (Tables S10 to S13). These results are satisfying and slightly better than those obtained from LC-MS/MS, demonstrating the high accuracy and reliability of the proposed method for the quantitative determination of multiple antibiotics in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual assay of antibiotics by experiment and image digitization using a color-scanning application
ce:section.ce:para: At present, expensive equipment and complicated operating procedures are often needed to realize rapid real-time antibiotic detection. Thus, it is essential to design a portable, facile, economical, and rapid method for the quantitative detection of antibiotics. Owing to the different FI and MEW responses of different types and concentrations of antibiotics, the dual-emission sensor array platform presented different fluorescence colors. The sensor array with the addition of TCs, AGs, and Qs exhibited green, yellow, and blue fluorescence under 312 nm irradiation from a UV lamp, respectively, and a clear color transformation was observed in the 96-well microplate with increasing concentration, as shown in Fig. S25. Furthermore, a color-scanning application downloaded online was used for chromaticity extraction and processing of the fluorescence color images to realize quantitative analysis and digitization of the fluorescent color. The R, G, and B values of the fluorescence colors were determined after spiking different concentrations (15 and 30 μM) of the nine single antibiotics into the prepared sensor array with a total of 144 multidimensional data to build the model. As demonstrated in Fig. 7, the accuracy of the SX-model was 100% at both 15 and 30 μM. The classification and concentration accuracies of nine unknown samples were both 100% (Tables S14 to S17). From these results, the simple, portable, and real-time detection method reported in this paper realizes the mobile, visual, and ultra-sensitive detection of antibiotics.

Traditional analytical methods have built separate models for individual concentrations, making it impossible to consider unknown samples and thus limiting their applicability in practical situations. Moreover, current approaches lack modules to describe the logical relationships between samples, which prevents the detection of unknown samples outside the dataset. We built an original generalized model and tested its performance in deionized water and real samples. As shown in Table S18, the 164 unknown samples have no concentration information, which resulted in them being passed to the inappropriate model and not being identified by LDA and SVM. Furthermore, traditional methods lack modules to describe the logical relationship between FI data and concentration. Hence, traditional methods cannot support sensor array detection of unknown samples. The SX-model has a unique classification model, wherein the 164 unknown samples were classified with 100% accuracy resulting in accurate concentration information. Furthermore, the mean concentration errors of unknown samples outside the dataset by the SX-model were 4.93%, 3.25%, and 4.43% for deionized water, river water, and milk samples, respectively (Tables S19 to S21).

In conventional array detection studies, binary and ternary mixed samples were identified separately. To examine the performance of our SX-model in complex environments, binary and ternary mixed samples were tested simultaneously in this study. The mean concentration errors were 6.20% and 11.11% for binary and ternary mixtures in deionized water outside the dataset, respectively (Tables S22).

Based on the aforementioned results, the SX-model is a powerful tool that supports dual-emission sensor arrays for the efficient detection of antibiotics in various complex environments. The SX-model has fundamentally surmounted the bottleneck of traditional methods in terms of practical applicability and the detection of unknown samples outside the datasets. Moreover, since the SX-model was established under the TPOT framework and is not a fixed model, it can be automatically adjusted to the best model based on the input data. Therefore, as an optimal recognition model, the SX-model is universal, and thus it has the potential to assist in array detection research and promote the development of array detection and analysis methods.

To summarize, a method for the quantitative detection of antibiotics in complex samples was innovatively constructed by adopting the stepwise prediction strategy combined with machine learning. Nine antibiotics were distinguished using a dual-emission sensor array based on P-CDs and CdTe QDs via different mechanisms, including IFE, static quenching, and electrostatic interactions. The SX-model, which combines stepwise prediction with machine learning under the framework of TPOT, selected ERF as the most accurate model and identified nine antibiotics at 0.5 μM. By combining the sensor array and SX-model, different concentrations of antibiotics were correctly discriminated in real samples. We further demonstrated the feasibility of this method for testing binary and ternary mixtures of antibiotics. In addition, the dual-emission sensor array showed a high sensitivity, adequate anti-interference, ability to detect antibiotics in complex media, and potential for visual analysis. More importantly, the SX-model was originally employed and evaluated in the field of array detection, with 100% accuracy in the classification and concentration detection of unknown samples in the datasets. Furthermore, the classification accuracy of unknown samples outside the datasets reached 100%, and the average concentration error was less than 5%, which surmounts the bottleneck of conventional methods related to samples outside the datasets. This study demonstrates the great potential of SX-model assisted dual-emission sensor arrays for the rapid, efficient, and visual detection of multiple antibiotics in a complex environment. In addition, the establishment of the original and universal SX-model will assist in array detection to face various challenges.
Key Technologies Research and Development Program (2016YFC0501205, 2016YFC0501208 and 2017YFD0200706), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775163).

Zijun Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Kejia Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Mengqian Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization. Tianhao Wang: Formal analysis. Xuejun Du: Methodology. Zideng Gao: Writing – review & editing. Shuwen Hu: Funding acquisition. Xueqin Ren: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition. Haojie Feng: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the program of Key Technologies Research and Development Program (2016YFC0501205, 2016YFC0501208 and 2017YFD0200706), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775163) express their deep thanks.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131590.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Fu, C. Long, L. Qin, Z. Jiang, T. Qing, P. Zhang, et al., Fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of tetracycline in wastewater based on heteroatoms-doped reduced state carbon dots, Environmental Pollution, 283(2021) 117109–117119.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive fluorometric determination of oxytetracycline based on carbon dots and Fe3O4 MNPs
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, P. Ni, S. Jiang, W. Lu, Z. Li, H. Liu, et al., Highly sensitive fluorometric determination of oxytetracycline based on carbon dots and Fe3O4 MNPs, Sensors and Actuators B-Chemical, 254(2018) 1118–1124.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A turn-on fluorescent sensor for selective and sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with gold nanoclusters based on inner filter effect
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, M. Li, Y. Xia, X. Ren, A turn-on fluorescent sensor for selective and sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase activity with gold nanoclusters based on inner filter effect, Acs Applied Materials & Interfaces, 9(2017) 120–126.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective fluorescence sensor sensing benzo a pyrene in water utilizing carbon dots derived from 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid
Chemosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Sun, Q. Zhou, X. Sheng, S. Li, Y. Tong, J. Guo, et al., Highly selective fluorescence sensor sensing benzo a pyrene in water utilizing carbon dots derived from 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid, Chemosphere, 282(2021) 131127–131127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-off-on fluorescent nanosensor for Fe3+ detection and cancer/normal cell differentiation via silicon-doped carbon quantum dots
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Gao, Y.-W. Jiang, H.-R. Jia, J. Yang, F.-G. Wu, On-off-on fluorescent nanosensor for Fe3+ detection and cancer/normal cell differentiation via silicon-doped carbon quantum dots, Carbon, 134(2018) 232–243.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive fluorescent detection of melamine in raw milk based on the inner filter effect of Au nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhang, X. Cao, H. Li, F. Guan, J. Guo, F. Shen, et al., Sensitive fluorescent detection of melamine in raw milk based on the inner filter effect of Au nanoparticles on the fluorescence of CdTe quantum dots, Food Chemistry, 135(2012) 1894–1900.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticle probe for colorimetric detection of aminoglycoside antibiotics: picomolar-level sensitivity toward streptomycin in water, serum, and milk samples
J. Sci. Food Agric.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Ghodake, S. Shinde, R.G. Saratale, A. Kadam, G.D. Saratale, A. Syed, et al., Silver nanoparticle probe for colorimetric detection of aminoglycoside antibiotics: picomolar-level sensitivity toward streptomycin in water, serum, and milk samples, Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture, 100(2020) 874–884.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zijun Xu graduated from Xinjiang University in 2018. Now, he is a Ph.D student supervised by Prof. Xueqin Ren at college of Resources and Environment Sciences, China Agricultural University. His research interests include the detection of environmental pollutants by fluorescent nanosensor arrays and machine learning data processing.
Kejia Wang received her bachelor's degree from China Agricultural University in 2021.

Mengqian Zhang is a master student supervised by Prof. Xueqin Ren at China Agricultural University. Currently, her research is focused on the application of fluorescence detection technology to oil pollution.

Tianhao Wang is a master student supervised by Prof. Xueqin Ren at China Agricultural University. Currently, his research is focused on the application of carbon quantum dots in agricultural production.
Xuejun Du is a Ph.D student supervised by Prof. Shuwen Hu at China Agricultural University. Currently, his research is focused on Fluorescence Detection Technology of Environmental pollutants.

Zideng Gao obtained his Ph. D from China Agricultural University in 2017. He was a postdoctoral at China Agricultural University. His research is mainly focused on the preparation of fluorescent nanomaterials based on agricultural waste.

Professor Shuwen Hu obtained his Ph. D from institute of Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences in 1999. He has been a professor at China Agricultural University. His research is mainly focused on the application of functional nanomaterials in improving crop stress resistance.

Professor Xueqin Ren obtained his Ph. D from Peking University in 1999. She was a postdoctoral fellow at the Medical University of South Carolina and the University of California, Irvine, from 1999 to 2005. She has been a professor at China Agricultural University. Her research is mainly focused on the application of novel fluorescence sensors in biosensing and environmental monitoring. Prof. Ren has over 30 publications, including Nat Commun., Angew Chem Int Edit., Anal Chem., Biosens Bioelectron., ACS Appl Mater Inter., Chem. Commun. Etc.

Haojie Feng obtained his Ph. D from China Agricultural University in 2019. He currently works at Shandong Agricultural University. His research is mainly focused on the application of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials in environmental monitoring.
